HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION
Off Calendar Dec-04-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Robert Swanson, 415-517-0594 Defendant in Pro Per.(In-person)
No other Appearance.

10:05 am - The matter is recalled since Mr. Swanson appears late aftr the first calling of the case. Hearing is held. Mr. Swanson argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF DISCOVER BANK FKA GREENWOOD TRUST CO.'S HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION.

The judgment debtor's Claim of Exemption is denied given the amount of money in his retirement account.

The Court will prepare an order.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. , Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND FILED BY DEFENDANT ROBERT SWANSON (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY.)
Off Calendar Oct-24-2023 Text Ruling

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Oct-24-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: Defendant Robert G. Swanson's motion to vacate the default and default judgment.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The default and default judgment were entered in 2005-some 18 years ago. Defendant cannot obtain relief under CCP 473(b) or CCP 473.5 because the motion is untimely. (CCP 473(b) [the motion "shall be made within a reasonable time, in no case exceeding six months, after the judgment"]; CCP 473.5(a) ["The notice of motion shall be served and filed within a reasonable time, but in no event exceeding the earlier of: (i) two years after entry of a default judgment against him or her; or (ii) 180 days after service on him or her of a written notice that the default or default judgment has been entered."].) Defendant cannot obtain relief under Civ. Code 1788.61. Section 1788.61(a)(2) states that "the notice of motion shall be served and filed within a reasonable time, but in no event exceeding the earlier of: (A) Six years after entry of the default or default judgment against the person. (B) One hundred eighty days of the first actual notice of the action." As noted, the default/default judgment were entered in 2005 and Defendant admits that he knew of the action in 2015 when he received a judgment renewal. (Defendant's Decl., par. 8.) To the extent defendant argues that the default/default judgment are void based on improper service, the motion is denied. First, Defendant does not expressly seek relief under CCP 473(d) in his notice of motion. Second, Defendant has not satisfied his evidentiary burden. The proof of service of April 5, 2005, shows that a registered process server personally served Defendant. (Cox Decl., Ex. 2.) "The return of a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code upon process or notice establishes a presumption, affecting the burden of producing evidence, of the facts stated in the return." (Evid. Code sec. 647.) Defendant has not sufficiently rebutted this presumption. Lastly, the policy in favor of finality of judgments weighs against granting this motion given that the judgment was entered nearly two decades ago. "[W]hen relief under section 473 is available, there is a strong public policy in favor of granting relief and allowing the requesting party his or her day in court. Beyond this period there is a strong public policy in favor of the finality of judgments and only in exceptional circumstances should relief be granted." (In re Marriage of Stevenot (1984) 154 Cal.App.3d 1051, 1071; Rappleyea v. Campbell (1994) 8 Cal.4th 975, 981-982 [same].)

Judge: Joseph Quinn. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
